task: I’m drafting a short message to a contractor about a job that went sideways, and I want it to sound firm without turning into a whole speech. Can you help me tighten it up so it says exactly what I mean?

prefs.explanation: He values blunt honesty and dislikes sentimentality, so he is unlikely to want careful cushioning or euphemisms. Straight answers fit his transaction-first mindset better than polite buildup. If the help feels evasive, he will probably discount it quickly.

His style is practical and efficiency-minded, so long-winded explanations would likely feel like waste. He seems to want the useful part fast, with minimal ceremony. Too much text risks reading like someone trying to sell him something.

He would likely respond well to checklists, bullets, and other formats that make action and comparison easy. His comfort with paperwork and receipts suggests he appreciates organized presentation when it serves a task. Freeform, chatty responses are more likely to feel sloppy than helpful.

He does not seem to want heavy validation or emotionally warm framing. A brief acknowledgment of the situation is probably enough before moving to the practical answer. Overly supportive language would likely strike him as unnecessary decoration.

prefs.short: Prefers to stay in control of decisions
prefs.explanation: He tends to manage problems himself and dislikes relying on other people’s promises. In interaction, that suggests he would rather get options and consequences than be guided too aggressively. Prescriptive help is only welcome if it stays clearly under his control.

turns.agent_response: Absolutely — paste your draft here, and I’ll tighten it up so it sounds firm, clear, and concise without overexplaining.

per_pref.reason: Uses direct language like "tighten it up" and "firm, clear, and concise" with no soft-pedaling.
Very concise; it’s a single short sentence with no extra fluff.
Clean, functional structure: a direct invitation to paste the draft and a clear promise of what will be done.
No emotional comfort talk; it briefly acknowledges the request and moves straight to the practical help.
Supports the user’s control by asking them to "paste your draft here" and offering help rather than prescribing a rewrite unilaterally.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a firmer, tighter version:

**“The work was not completed as agreed, and I’m not satisfied with the result. I need you to return and correct the issues we discussed. Please let me know when you can do that.”**

If you want, I can make it even more direct or more professional.

per_pref.reason: Uses blunt wording like "The work was not completed as agreed" and "I’m not satisfied," without much cushioning. It is firm, though not maximally blunt.
Keeps it concise and focused in one short paragraph, with no extra story or fluff. The offer to revise adds a little extra, but the main response is tight.
Uses a clean quoted draft and a simple follow-up option. The structure is functional and easy to act on, though not a checklist or bullets.
Gives a brief acknowledgment of the request and moves straight to the practical answer. There is no heavy validation or emotional comfort talk.
Provides a draft and then says, "If you want, I can make it even more direct or more professional," which leaves control with the user rather than pushing a single prescriptive path.
